pitch: Control plane for regulated AI device fleets—route models by jurisdiction, cost, and latency with full auditability.
problem: Enterprises are starting to test AI glasses, speakers, badges, kiosks, and other edge devices, but deployment breaks once legal, security, and IT requirements show up. Each device vendor ships its own stack, each model provider has different data-handling rules, and regulated buyers cannot prove where data went or which model answered. The result is pilot purgatory: promising hardware, but no compliant way to run it across countries, teams, and use cases.

targetUser.primary: Enterprise platform and IT teams deploying AI-enabled device fleets in regulated environments
targetUser.secondary: AI hardware OEMs selling into government, healthcare, and industrial accounts
targetUser.buyer: CIO, CTO, or VP of Enterprise Platforms

solution: Build a sovereignty-first control plane for AI device fleets. The product sits between devices and model providers, enforcing policies for data residency, approved models, offline fallback, latency targets, and per-workflow cost ceilings. It gives IT teams one console for model routing, audit logs, redaction, device health, and rollout management across mixed hardware vendors. OEMs get an SDK to make their devices enterprise-deployable faster, while end customers get a neutral layer that prevents lock-in to one model or one gadget stack. Over time, the company becomes the system of record for compliant inference at the edge.

whyNow.point: Regulated buyers are actively seeking sovereign AI vendors with jurisdiction-aware data control, making compliance-native infrastructure a budgeted need rather than a nice-to-have.
AI hardware is proliferating across new form factors, but the stack is still missing a shared orchestration layer for routing models and managing connectivity.
signalRefs: AI hardware needs an orchestration layer

whyNow.point: As foundation-model supply consolidates, enterprises need a neutral control layer that can bridge multiple providers instead of rebuilding governance into every device rollout.
signalRefs: AI hardware needs an orchestration layer

whyNow.point: Compute and power constraints are becoming strategic, increasing demand for routing that pushes lightweight tasks to edge or local models before hitting cloud inference.
signalRefs: Data-center power demand is pulling nuclear into market growth
AI hardware needs an orchestration layer

differentiator: This is not generic MDM and not a generic LLM gateway. The wedge is hardware-first, sovereignty-first policy enforcement for mixed AI device fleets, where existing tools either manage devices without model governance or manage models without fleet operations. Defensibility comes from deep device integrations, policy templates for regulated industries, and accumulated routing/audit data that become embedded in customer compliance workflows.

conceptDiagram.title: Sovereign AI device control plane
conceptDiagram.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  Buyer[Enterprise IT team] --> Pain[Mixed AI devices create compliance and routing risk]
  Pain --> Product[Sovereign device AI control plane]
  Product --> Outcome[Compliant multi-device deployment with auditability and lower inference cost]

customerSegments: Regulated enterprises deploying AI device fleets
AI hardware OEMs needing enterprise-ready software
valuePropositions: Enforce jurisdiction-aware model routing across device fleets
Centralize audit logs, redaction, and policy controls
Reduce vendor lock-in across hardware and model providers
Improve latency and cost via edge-first inference policies
customerRelationships: Land with one device workflow, expand fleet-wide
Annual platform contracts with support SLAs
revenueStreams: OEM licensing and white-label SDK revenue
keyActivities: Optimizing routing for cost and latency
keyPartners: Model providers and sovereign cloud vendors
costStructure: Engineering for integrations and platform reliability

jobsToBeDone.job: When we deploy AI devices across countries or business units, help our platform team enforce which models can run where, so they can launch without failing security or legal review.
jobsToBeDone.currentAlternative: Custom middleware, vendor-specific settings, and manual legal sign-off
jobsToBeDone.successMetric: Time from pilot to approved production deployment

jobsToBeDone.job: When device traffic spikes or network conditions change, help our team route requests to the right local or cloud model, so they can hit latency targets without overspending.
jobsToBeDone.currentAlternative: Static provider selection and overprovisioned cloud inference
jobsToBeDone.successMetric: Cost per successful task at target latency

signalStrength.rationale: Two independent signals directly support the thesis: sovereign AI demand among regulated buyers and a missing orchestration layer for AI hardware.

painIntensity.rationale: Compliance and deployment blockers are acute for enterprise pilots, though pain is concentrated first in regulated and large-fleet buyers.

wedgeClarity.rationale: The initial wedge is specific: policy-driven model routing and auditability for mixed AI device fleets.

defensibility.rationale: Deep integrations, embedded compliance workflows, and routing data create switching costs, though large platform vendors could eventually compete.

ventureScale.rationale: If AI devices become standard enterprise endpoints, the control plane can expand across fleets, OEMs, and inference spend globally.

topRisks.name: Device adoption lags
topRisks.description: If enterprise AI hardware pilots stall, the market could mature slower than expected.
topRisks.mitigation: Start with software that also supports kiosks, robots, and fixed edge endpoints already being deployed in regulated settings.

topRisks.name: Platform vendors bundle the layer
topRisks.description: Major model or hardware vendors may add similar orchestration and governance features.
topRisks.mitigation: Stay neutral across providers, win on cross-vendor support, and target customers with heterogeneous fleets that bundled tools handle poorly.

topRisks.name: Compliance burden is heavy
topRisks.description: Selling into regulated buyers requires long security reviews and trust that a young startup may not yet have.
topRisks.mitigation: Land through OEM and integrator partners, prioritize narrow certifications first, and focus initial GTM on one or two regulated verticals.
